---
title: "genepicoloc: A Kidney Genomics Colocalization Atlas Integrating Proteomics, Metabolomics, and Clinical Phenotypes"
author:
  - name: Oleg Borisov
    affiliations:
      - Institute of Genetic Epidemiology, Faculty of Medicine and Medical Center, University of Freiburg, Germany
    orcid: 0000-0001-8700-4335
    corresponding: true
    email: oleg.borisov@uniklinik-freiburg.de
  - name: "[co-authors TBD]"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  docx:
    reference-doc: /home/oleg/Work/bioinfo/tools/custom-reference.docx
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Abstract

Genetic colocalization analysis identifies shared causal variants between traits,
enabling molecular interpretation of GWAS loci. However, systematic application
across diverse molecular and clinical datasets remains challenging due to data
heterogeneity, computational scale, and lack of integrated visualization tools.
Here we present genepicoloc, an R package for scalable colocalization analysis
with a standalone implementation of the approximate Bayes factor (ABF) method,
and apply it to build a comprehensive kidney genomics colocalization atlas.

Using CKDGen round 4 GWAS for six kidney function traits (eGFR, BUN, UACR,
microalbuminuria, urate, gout), we performed 22,667 high-confidence
colocalizations (PP.H4 > 0.8) across 553 genomic regions against 11 datasets
encompassing plasma proteomics (UKB PPP, deCODE), metabolomics (GCKD plasma and
urine), clinical phenotypes (MVP, FinnGen, UKB TOPMed), kidney-specific gene
expression, and kidney MRI volumes. We find that plasma protein QTLs provide
the strongest colocalization signal for kidney traits (14,710 colocalizations),
substantially outperforming blood eQTLs (1,194), and identify 136 loci where
eGFR-associated variants also determine kidney structure as measured by MRI.
The atlas is accessible through an interactive Shiny application enabling
network visualization and regional association plots.

# Introduction

## Genetic colocalization in the post-GWAS era

- GWAS identifies loci but not mechanisms
- Colocalization tests whether two traits share a causal variant at a locus
- ABF method [@giambartolomei2014] is the standard approach
- GPMap [@elmore2026] provides broad pleiotropy mapping - we complement with kidney-specific depth

## The kidney genomics opportunity

- CKDGen consortium: largest kidney function GWAS (N > 1M for eGFR)
- Rich molecular data available: plasma proteomics, metabolomics, kidney eQTL
- Unique datasets: kidney MRI volumes, GCKD cohort metabolomics
- No existing kidney-focused colocalization resource

## Gaps addressed

- Scalable pipeline for colocalization across heterogeneous data sources
- Standalone ABF implementation without dependency on the coloc package
- Integration of kidney-specific datasets (MRI volumes, kidney eQTL, metabolomics)
- Interactive visualization for exploring molecular chains

# Methods

## Standalone ABF colocalization

- Clean-room implementation from published equations [@giambartolomei2014; @wallace2020]
- Zero external dependencies (base R only)
- Verified exact match with coloc::coloc.abf() across 25+ real-data tests
- ~80 lines of code: `compute_labf()`, `combine_abf()`, `coloc_abf()`

## Preprocessing pipeline

- `genepi_liftover()`: hg19 to hg38 coordinate conversion
- `name_by_position()`: standardized variant naming via Ensembl VCFs
- `handle_underflow()`: p-value precision for extreme significance (p < 1e-324)
- `match_cols()`: column standardization across diverse GWAS formats

## Scalable architecture

- Region detection with `get_coloc_regions()` (configurable window, threshold)
- Job-based parallelization: regions split into jobs, datasets processed in parallel
- Tar-based storage for efficient I/O with thousands of result files
- YAML-based study configuration for reproducible analyses

## Input data

### Query GWAS (CKDGen round 4)

| Trait | Publication | Type | N |
|-------|------------|------|---|
| eGFR | Wuttke et al. 2019 | Quantitative | ~1.2M |
| BUN | Wuttke et al. 2019 | Quantitative | ~243K |
| UACR | Teumer et al. 2019 | Quantitative | ~547K |
| Microalbuminuria | Teumer et al. 2019 | Case-control | ~52K |
| Urate | Tin et al. 2019 | Quantitative | ~289K |
| Gout | Tin et al. 2019 | Case-control | ~13K |

### Target datasets (sumstats_2)

| Category | Study | N datasets | Description |
|----------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| pQTL | UKB PPP EUR | 2,940 | Olink plasma proteomics |
| pQTL | deCODE/Icelanders | 4,907 | SomaScan plasma proteomics |
| PheWAS | MVP EUR | 1,669 | Million Veteran Program |
| PheWAS | FinnGen r9 | 2,272 | Finnish clinical endpoints |
| PheWAS | UKB TOPMed | 1,419 | UK Biobank phenotypes |
| mQTL | GCKD plasma | 1,297 | Metabolon plasma metabolomics |
| mQTL | GCKD urine | 1,409 | Metabolon urine metabolomics |
| eQTL | eQTLGen | 1 | Blood eQTL (N=31,684) |
| eQTL | Kidney eQTL | 5 | Susztak kidney tissue |
| GWAS | CKDGen r4 | 6 | Cross-kidney-trait coloc |
| Imaging | UKB kidney MRI | 5 | Kidney volume phenotypes |

## Visualization

- Interactive Shiny application with network visualization (visNetwork)
- Region-centered view: genomic region -> colocalized traits
- Trait-centered view: selected trait -> genomic regions
- Regional association plots (LocusZoom-style) with plotly
- Gene annotation panel (NCBI, Reactome, HPO)

# Results

## Atlas overview

- 6 CKDGen traits x 11 studies x ~16,000 datasets
- 22,667 high-confidence colocalizations (PP.H4 > 0.8)
- 553 unique genomic regions
- Processing time: ~17 hours per trait on 20 cores

### Colocalizations by data type

| Data type | Colocalizations | % of total |
|-----------|-----------------|------------|
| pQTL (UKB PPP + deCODE) | 10,710 | 47% |
| PheWAS (MVP + FinnGen + UKB TOPMed) | 9,021 | 40% |
| mQTL (GCKD plasma + urine) | 717 | 3% |
| eQTL (eQTLGen + Kidney eQTL) | 1,194 | 5% |
| GWAS (CKDGen r4) | 889 | 4% |
| Imaging (kidney MRI) | 136 | 1% |

**Key finding:** Plasma protein QTLs provide the strongest molecular signal for
kidney GWAS interpretation, substantially outperforming blood eQTLs. This
reflects the kidney-specific nature of these GWAS signals - kidney genes are
not expressed in blood.

## Showcase region 1: UMOD/PDILT (chr16)

- 944 colocalizations across eGFR, BUN, urate
- 8 studies contributing
- Kidney MRI volumes: all 4 phenotypes colocalize (PP.H4 = 1.0)
- 232 plasma proteins colocalize (trans-pQTL effects)
- Kidney eQTL confirms regulatory effect
- Known biology: uromodulin, most abundant urinary protein, kidney tubule marker

## Showcase region 2: GCKR (chr2)

- 4,368 colocalizations (largest region in the atlas)
- All 6 kidney traits colocalize - the metabolic hub
- 10 of 11 studies contribute
- Extensive metabolomics signal (GCKD plasma + urine)
- Known biology: glucokinase regulatory protein, central metabolic regulator

## Showcase region 3: SHROOM3 (chr4)

- 216 colocalizations across eGFR, BUN, UACR, MA
- Notably absent: urate and gout (kidney-specific, not urate pathway)
- Kidney MRI: 9 volume colocalizations (cortex, fullvol, TKV)
- pQTL: 69 proteins, including kidney structure markers
- Known biology: actin-binding protein, podocyte cytoskeleton, glomerular development

## Showcase region 4: SLC2A9 (chr4)

- 27 colocalizations specific to eGFR, urate, gout
- Clean specificity: does NOT colocalize with UACR, MA, or BUN
- Known biology: major urate transporter (URAT1), uricosuric drug target

## Cross-trait architecture

- GCKR region shared by all 6 kidney traits
- UMOD/PDILT shared by eGFR + BUN + urate (function + structure)
- SHROOM3 shared by eGFR + BUN + UACR + MA (glomerular traits only)
- SLC2A9 shared by eGFR + urate + gout (urate pathway only)
- Clear biological clustering: glomerular vs tubular vs metabolic pathways

## Kidney MRI volumes as a unique colocalization layer

- 136 high-confidence colocalizations across 39 genomic regions
- STC1 (stanniocalcin-1): only locus colocalizing with all 5 volume phenotypes
- UMOD/PDILT: 4 of 5 volumes (no hilus)
- BCAS3: 4 of 5 volumes, shared across eGFR + gout + urate
- These are kidney-structure-determining loci not captured by any molecular assay

## Blood eQTL vs kidney eQTL

- eQTLGen (blood, N=31,684): 89 colocalizations for eGFR
- Kidney eQTL (N=73-686): 498 colocalizations for eGFR
- Despite 50x smaller sample size, kidney eQTL has 5.6x more colocalizations
- Highlights tissue-specificity of kidney GWAS signals
- Caveat: kidney eQTL uses p < 1e-5 threshold (may include false positives)

# Discussion

## Proteomics as the dominant molecular layer for kidney genetics

- pQTL accounts for 47% of all colocalizations
- This contrasts with the conventional eQTL-first approach
- Explanation: kidney genes are not expressed in blood; proteins circulate
- Implication: plasma proteomics should be prioritized for kidney GWAS follow-up

## Kidney MRI volumes: a unique structural phenotype

- Only 136 colocalizations but extremely high biological value
- Identifies loci where genetic variants affect both kidney function AND structure
- Not available in any other colocalization resource (GPMap, Open Targets)
- Potential for identifying therapeutic targets that preserve kidney structure

## Comparison with GPMap

- GPMap: 49.3M colocalization pairs, 16K traits, broad scope
- genepicoloc: 22,667 focused on kidney, with unique datasets
- Complementary: GPMap for broad pleiotropy, genepicoloc for kidney depth
- Unique to genepicoloc: GCKD metabolomics, kidney MRI volumes, kidney eQTL

## Standalone coloc implementation

- Zero-dependency ABF computation (~80 lines of R)
- Exact match with coloc::coloc.abf() verified
- Reduces installation complexity (coloc has multiple dependencies)
- Enables lightweight deployment and CRAN submission

## Limitations

- Single causal variant assumption (ABF, not SuSiE-based fine-mapping)
- Kidney eQTL data has p < 1e-5 threshold limitation
- CKDGen r4 GWAS are European-ancestry predominant
- Regional plots require substantial disk space (50GB+ for full atlas)

## Data and code availability

- R package: https://github.com/genepi-freiburg/genepicoloc
- Interactive browser: [URL TBD]
- Atlas results: [repository TBD]
- All CKDGen input data publicly available from GWAS Catalog

# References

::: {#refs}
:::

# Supplementary Material

## Supplementary Table 1: Complete atlas statistics by trait and study

## Supplementary Table 2: All 136 kidney MRI volume colocalizations

## Supplementary Table 3: Cross-trait shared regions

## Supplementary Figure 1: Genome-wide colocalization landscape for all 6 traits

## Supplementary Figure 2: Sensitivity analysis - PP.H4 threshold comparison
